elements.sent-2411-001: We analyze gravitaxis of a Brownian circle swimmer by deriving and characterizing analytically the experimentally measurable intermediate scattering function (ISF).
elements.sent-2411-002: To solve the associated Fokker-Planck equation we use a spectral-theory approach and find formal expressions in terms of eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the overdamped-noisy-driven-pendulum problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: We further perform a Taylor series of the ISF in the wavevector to read off the cumulants up to the fourth order.
elements.sent-2411-004: We focus on the skewness and kurtosis analyzed for four observation directions in the 2D-plane.
elements.sent-2411-005: Validating our findings involves conducting Langevin-dynamics simulations and interpreting the results using a harmonic approximation.
elements.sent-2411-006: The skewness and kurtosis are amplified as the orienting torque approaches the intrinsic angular drift of the circle swimmer from above, highlighting deviations from Gaussian behavior.
elements.sent-2411-007: Transforming the ISF to the comoving frame, again a measurable quantity, reveals gravitactic effects and diverse behaviors spanning from diffusive motion at low wavenumbers to circular motion at intermediate and directed motion at higher wavenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-008: Microswimmers, microscopic objects capable of moving in liquid environments, have attracted considerable interest because of their important role in nature, such as in fertilization processes~\cite{Nosrati_2015, schwarz2020sperm, debnath2020enhanced , or in diverse biomedical applications, for example, micro motors~\cite{Baraban_2012, Jahir_2013, Koumakis_2013, Liu_2020 , imaging~\cite{pane2019imaging, hosseini2020high , microsurgery~\cite{vyskocil2020cancer , targeted drug delivery~\cite{Yan_2022, Mahon_2012,Tottori_2012 , and many more~\cite{bunea2020recent .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Of interest is their nontrivial dynamics, stemming from their intrinsic state of being out of equilibrium because of the ongoing conversion of energy into directed motion.
elements.sent-2411-010: Microswimmers can be found in a variety of systems, both biological, such as bacteria, sperm cells, and microorganisms, and artificially synthesized, including Janus particles, magnetic microswimmers, and active colloids~\cite{kurzthaler2024characterization,Zhiyu_2021,Bechinger_2016,Elgeti_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In particular, circle swimmers move in curved trajectories via interactions with their physical shape, propulsion mechanism, symmetry, or external interactions ~\cite{Liebchen_2017, Kuemmel_2013, lowen2016chirality, BorgetenHagen_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Taxis, in all its forms, embodies the ability of organisms and particles to actively respond to external stimuli, for instance, this capability could enable microorganisms to avoid sedimentation or adjust to the intensity of the light they are exposed to.
elements.sent-2411-013: Their guided motions can be influenced by external factors, such as chemical gradients, light intensity, magnetic fields, fluid flows, or gravity - referred to as gravitaxis.
elements.sent-2411-014: Negative gravitaxis, which is the response to move oppositely to the gravitational field has been observed for organisms, such as Euglena gracilis~\cite{Lebert_1999 , Paramecium~\cite{Hemmersbach_2001 or asymmetric self-propelled colloidal particles~\cite{BorgetenHagen_2014 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Theoretical modeling and experiments on these colloidal particles demonstrated their alignment because of gravitational forces, forming the foundation for our current model.
elements.sent-2411-016: In a previous study~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 , two of the present authors used the L-shaped gravitactic circle swimmer from Ref.~\cite{BorgetenHagen_2014 and mapped it to an overdamped noisy driven pendulum.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This allowed computing analytically the variance and diffusivity, which revealed a resonant diffusivity when the orienting torque approaches the intrinsic angular drift of the circle swimmer~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: While the variance provides insight into transport properties ranging from directed and circular motion to diffusive behavior, extracting further information by computing higher moments becomes increasingly more tedious.
elements.sent-2411-019: For the case of vanishing external force, higher-order moments and correlation functions have been derived analytically using a Laplace transform-based method applied to the Fokker-Planck equation~\cite{Kurzthaler_2017,pattanayak2024impact .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The ISF encodes all moments in terms of derivatives with respect to the wave vector, providing full spatio-temporal information and has been computed analytically for an anisotropic active Brownian particle~\cite{Kurzthaler_2016,Kurzthaler_2018_janus and Brownian circle swimmer~\cite{Kurzthaler_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, the ISF is directly accessible via experiments, for example, dynamic light scattering~\cite{VanMegen_1991 , single particle tracking~\cite{Kurzthaler_2018_janus or differential dynamic microscopy (DDM)~\cite{Cerbino_2008,Wilson_2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this work, we shall analytically derive the ISF for the model of a circle swimmer subjected to gravity, as described in Ref.~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 across various length and time scales.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We analyze the gravitactic effects by comparing different strengths of the orienting torque relative to the intrinsic angular drift.
elements.sent-2411-024: To validate our findings, we will conduct Langevin-dynamics simulations and rationalize the results in terms of a harmonic approximation, focusing on various regimes near the bifurcation point.
elements.sent-2411-025: We solve the corresponding Fokker-Planck equation in Fourier space by using an operator representation and a spectral-theory approach.
elements.sent-2411-026: The analytical expressions involve the eigenvalues and eigenfunctions of this operator, which can be computed numerically.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, we derive analytically the skewness and kurtosis by time-dependent perturbation theory.
elements.sent-2411-028: This method has previously demonstrated to be applicable in various contexts, such as the anisotropic active Brownian particle~\cite{Kurzthaler_2016 or the anisotropic Brownian circle swimmer~\cite{Kurzthaler_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In our current work, we extend this analytical framework to encompass microswimmers navigating an external field, with a specific focus on the phenomenon of gravitaxis~\cite{BorgetenHagen_2014,Chepizhko_2022 .}

The harmonic approximation (HA) for the case of $\gamma /\omega = 2$ is shown in dashed lines. The inset is a zoom close to the inflection point.} \label{fig:washboard_potential}
elements.sent-2411-030: An active L-shaped particle moving in the presence of an external gravitational field causing a torque has been introduced in Ref.~\cite{BorgetenHagen_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: It has been demonstrated that the asymmetry in its shape alone was sufficient to induce gravitactic behavior.
elements.sent-2411-032: We use a simplified model where noise terms and additional drift components associated with (anisotropic) translational diffusion are omitted.
elements.sent-2411-033: In principle, one can include these terms as outlined in the Supplemental Material of Ref.~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 , however they do not qualitatively affect the circular motion of interest for discussing gravitaxis.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The particle's position in space \begin{equation}\label{eq:positioneom} \dot{r}(t)= v u (t)=v  \cos \vartheta(t) \sin \vartheta(t)  ,\end{equation} evolves in a plane with constant speed $v$ along its orientation parametrized by an angle $\vartheta$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The angular dynamics are governed by the equation of motion \begin{equation}\label{eq:thetaeom} \dot{\vartheta }(t) = \omega - \gamma \sin \vartheta (t) + \zeta (t).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-036: Here $\omega >0$ denotes the constant angular drift because of the shape asymmetry.
elements.sent-2411-037: The gravitational force acting on the particle translates to an orienting torque, $\gamma \propto g, \gamma > 0$, via the translational-rotational coupling, see the Supplemental Material of Ref.~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Furthermore, $\zeta (t)$ corresponds to a centered Gaussian white noise with delta-correlated variance $\langle \zeta (t) \zeta (t^\prime) \rangle = 2 D_\text{rot \delta (t-t^\prime)$, where $D_\text{rot}$ is the rotational diffusion coefficient.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Strictly speaking, $\gamma$ has dimensions of a drift term, nevertheless we refer to it as a torque since it tends to align the L-shaped particle because of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-040: As a peculiarity, gravity causes the angle $\vartheta$ to align in the $x$-direction perpendicularly to the gravitational field.
elements.sent-2411-041: The L-shaped particle is oriented along $\vartheta$ and an additional Hall angle, similar to the Hall effect~\cite{BorgetenHagen_2014,Chepizhko_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: There is an instructive mechanical analogy to the overdamped noisy driven pendulum~\cite{stratonovich_1967_topics, gitterman_2008_noisy .}
elements.sent-2411-043: The only difference is that the angle in the pendulum problem is measured from the negative $y$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this analogy, $\omega$ represents a driving motor trying to increase the angle at a constant rate.
elements.sent-2411-045: In contrast, gravity parametrized by $\gamma $ aligns the pendulum to a downward position.
elements.sent-2411-046: The deterministic part of the angular motion in \cref{eq:thetaeom derives from an effective potential \begin{equation}\label{eq: washboard_potential} \frac{D_rot}{k_BT} U(\vartheta)= - \omega \vartheta - \gamma \cos \vartheta ,\end{equation} see \cref{fig:washboard_potential .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: As all angles are $2 \pi$-periodic, periodic boundary conditions are applied.
elements.sent-2411-048: For $\gamma > \omega $ the potential displays a local minimum located at $\vartheta_* = \arcsin {(\omega /\gamma ) }\in [0, \pi/2]$.
elements.sent-2411-049: Similarly, the potential exhibits a maximum at $\pi - \vartheta_*$, yielding a barrier height $\Delta U = U(\pi - \vartheta_*)- U(\vartheta ^*) $.
elements.sent-2411-050: If $\gamma =\omega $, the potential displays an inflection point.
elements.sent-2411-051: In the absence of noise, this point corresponds to a saddle-node bifurcation.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the pendulum analogy, above this point ($ \gamma > \omega $), there is a stable fixed point at $\vartheta_*$ where the angle is locked.
elements.sent-2411-053: In contrast, below the bifurcation point ($\gamma < \omega$), no fixed points exist and the motor drives the pendulum to complete full rotations.
elements.sent-2411-054: Correspondingly, we refer to $\gamma > \omega$ as the locked state and to $\gamma < \omega$ as the running state~\cite{strogatz2018nonlinear .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The introduction of noise smears this transition.
elements.sent-2411-056: Another special case that is analytically tractable is the Brownian circle swimmer, i.e., without the orienting torque, which has been already fully characterized~\cite{Kurzthaler_2017,gitterman_2008_noisy .}

elements.sent-2411-194: In this work, we extend the analysis on the total variance as discussed in Ref.~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Our investigation includes four directions of observation: the $x$ and $y$ axes, as well as the directions parallel and perpendicular to the mean velocity, see \cref{fig:6 .}
elements.sent-2411-196: We note that the total variance can be computed by summing the $x$ and $y$ directions or the parallel and perpendicular directions.
elements.sent-2411-197: Along these directions, we explore various values of the orienting torque $\gamma $, spanning ranges above, exactly at, and below the classical bifurcation.
elements.sent-2411-198: For small times we find persistent growth that is quadratic in time $\propto t^2$, followed by oscillations starting at times $t \gtrsim \tau_\omega $ for values $\gamma $ below the bifurcation, similar to a free circle swimmer~\cite{Kurzthaler_2017,Kuemmel_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-199: The oscillations are fading out for long times as the angular motion is randomized, and the particle starts to show diffusive behavior for $t \gtrsim \tau_\text{rot $, indicated by a linear increase in time.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The curves for the case below the classical bifurcation are similar in all four directions, indicating that in the running phase the diffusion becomes isotropic.
elements.sent-2411-201: At and above the bifurcation we observe diffusive behavior and no oscillations occur as the angle $\vartheta$ is locked by the strong gravitactic torque.
elements.sent-2411-202: Close to the classical bifurcation, $\gamma = \omega$, the diffusivity, i.e., the slope in the long-time regime, is enhanced, as recently discussed in Ref.~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: For the case of a strong orienting torque $\gamma >\omega$, the prefactor of diffusive growth $\propto t$ is much bigger for the perpendicular direction than for the parallel direction, i.e., the particle diffuses mainly in the direction perpendicular to the mean motion.
elements.sent-2411-204: In the harmonic approximation this becomes exact as the diffusion coefficient, $D_n=0$, evaluates to zero for the parallel direction, compare \cref{eq:HA_variance .}
elements.sent-2411-205: The approximation coincides with the curves, the better, the stronger the orienting torque.
elements.sent-2411-206: For the case of $\gamma =1.05 \omega$, the harmonic approximation does not match the analytic curve.
elements.sent-2411-207: The reason has been identified in~\cite{Chepizhko_2022 as the particle hops over the barrier before relaxing in the minimum.}
elements.sent-2411-208: In conclusion, we find that the particle in the locked phase diffuses primarily in perpendicular direction, while in this direction the mean velocity is zero.
elements.sent-2411-209: The closer the particle's mean velocity is to the maximum $v$, the lower is its diffusivity, which can be seen in the parallel direction.
elements.sent-2411-210: In the periodic running phase, the diffusivity becomes isotropic and the mean velocity increases close to the bifurcation.

elements.sent-2411-250: We have investigated gravitaxis of a Brownian circle swimmer in two dimensions by deriving an analytical expression of the experimentally measurable ISF and expanded the ISF in its cumulants to obtain the mean velocity, variance, skewness, and kurtosis.
elements.sent-2411-251: The observables are analyzed in four directions of interest: parallel to the gravitational force $y$ and perpendicular to that in $x$-direction, as well as parallel and perpendicular to the mean velocity, referred to as parallel and perpendicular direction.
elements.sent-2411-252: The solutions are obtained by a spectral-theoretical approach and validated by Langevin-dynamics simulations.
elements.sent-2411-253: First, the model we use maps the particle's orientation to an overdamped noisy driven pendulum with a classical bifurcation when the orientational torque reaches the angular drift.
elements.sent-2411-254: This picture suggests an analysis of different orientational torques below (running phase), exactly at and above (locked phase) the classical bifurcation.
elements.sent-2411-255: We have interpreted our results in terms of a harmonic approximation, which is accurate, especially for a small diffusion coefficient and fixed torque above and not too close to the bifurcation.
elements.sent-2411-256: In this regime the harmonic approximation is in good agreement with the numerically computed ISF and variance.
elements.sent-2411-257: We show the first eigenvalues and they coincide with the eigenvalues of the harmonic approximation within this regime and identified symmetries of the eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
elements.sent-2411-258: Second, we have analyzed the ISF for different orientational torques and wave vectors $\mathbf{k v /\omega$ and demonstrated that transforming the ISF into the comoving frame eliminates oscillations caused by pure drift motion stemming from the non equilibrium dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-259: In the running phase, the dynamics are similar to the Brownian circle swimmer, with the difference that the imaginary part is highly oscillating, especially in the parallel direction .
elements.sent-2411-260: We observed persistent motion at large wavenumbers, circular motion at intermediate wavenumbers, and enhanced diffusion at small wavenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-261: Close to the bifurcation, oscillation frequency increase, with the fastest diffusion at small wavenumbers and persistent motion at intermediate wavenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-262: In the locked phase, because of the mapping to the comoving frame, we found diffusion, that was magnitudes faster in perpendicular direction and the faster for higher wavenumbers.
elements.sent-2411-263: Additionally, we have investigated the variance and mean motion, as well as the skewness and kurtosis, for various directions and different gravitational torques.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the running phase, the mean velocity in parallel direction is becoming significant close to the bifurcation as the swimmer oscillates for very low gravitational torques, i.e., does not move in a certain direction.
elements.sent-2411-265: In perpendicular direction the velocity is, by definition, zero.
elements.sent-2411-266: The variance, skewness and kurtosis exhibit oscillations, at times comparable to the time it takes the particle to complete a circle.
elements.sent-2411-267: These oscillations are more pronounced the lower the gravitational torque and independent of the observation direction.

elements.sent-2411-270: In the variance we observe persistent, quadratic growth in time until the particle begins oscillating.
elements.sent-2411-271: For times when the particle has completed many oscillations, it exhibits diffusive behavior.
elements.sent-2411-272: The skewness and kurtosis reach their highest values close but above the bifurcation, indicating big deviations from the harmonic approximation for those values.

elements.sent-2411-312: Differential dynamic microscopy (DDM) has been first introduced by Cerbino and Trappe~\cite{Cerbino_2008 to measure the ISF experimentally, relying only on advanced imaging techniques, rather than scattering experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-313: The theoretical background of DDM is nicely presented in Refs.~\cite{Giavazzi_2009, Wilson_2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-314: This technique can also be adapted to the comoving frame, indicated in the literature as well~\cite{edera2021deformation, giavazzi2014digital, richards2021particle .}
elements.sent-2411-315: In the DDM method, a sequence of microscopic images is taken, and dynamic contrast is measured.
elements.sent-2411-316: In the comoving frame, the image is shifted with velocity $v$ to include a drift and so the difference of the two images recorded at time $t$ and $0$ is \begin{equation}\Delta I (r, t ) = I (r, t ) - I (r- v t, 0 ).\end{equation}.
